schools.summary: The multiplying of mankind: 'and it came to pass, when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them' — mankind multiplying rapidly (the long-lived antediluvians, ch. 5, peopling the earth), daughters born among the increasing population (the verse setting the scene for the intermarriages, v.2, that led to the universal corruption, v.5, and the flood); the race greatly increased since Adam, the earth filling with people (the multiplication of mankind, blessed by God, 1:28, now becoming the occasion of widespread wickedness as the line of Seth and the line of Cain mingle); 'daughters were born unto them' (the birth of daughters noted because their beauty, v.2, drew the sons of God, the verse preparing for the corrupting intermarriages, the increase of the race and the birth of fair daughters setting the stage for the apostasy and corruption that brought on the flood) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: when men began to multiply on the face of the earth, and daughters were born unto them
the multiplying of mankind and the birth of daughters
the race increasing, the line of Seth and Cain mingling
the scene set for the corrupting intermarriages

schools.summary: Calvin and Gill (the Sethite view, the dominant Protestant reading): the 'sons of God' are the godly line of Seth (the worshippers of God, 4:26), and the 'daughters of men' the ungodly line of Cain; the apostasy lying in the godly intermarrying with the ungodly, choosing wives by mere beauty ('they took them wives of all which they chose'), mingling the holy seed with the profane, and so corrupting the church; the unequal yoking of the children of God with the children of the world the root of the universal corruption (v.5) that brought on the flood, the godly seduced from their religion by marriage with the wicked.

Keil and Delitzsch (the angelic view): the 'sons of God' are angels (fallen angels), who took human wives — the ancient/Enochic interpretation, supported by 1 Peter 3:19-20; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6 (the angels who 'kept not their first estate'); the union of angelic beings with women producing the Nephilim (v.4). Gill and Clarke note this view but object that Christ says the angels neither marry nor are given in marriage (Matthew 22:30). A third view (the Jewish, and some) takes the 'sons of God' as the nobles, great men, or dynastic rulers (the powerful, the kings), and the 'daughters of men' as women of lower rank, the great men taking many wives by lust and tyranny.

debates.question: Who are the 'sons of God' who took the daughters of men?

sides.position: The godly line of Seth, intermarrying with the ungodly Cainites (the dominant Protestant view)
Fallen angels, taking human wives (the angelic view; 1 Peter 3:19; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 6)
The nobles, great men, or dynastic rulers (the Jewish reading)

themes: the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose
the sons of God taking wives of the daughters of men
the crux of the sons of God (Sethites, fallen angels, or nobles)
the corrupting intermarriage that brought on the flood

schools.summary: God's word and the 120 years: 'and the LORD said, My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years' — Henry and Clarke read 'strive' as the Spirit's contending with man (by conscience, by the preaching of Noah, a preacher of righteousness, 2 Peter 2:5, and by God's patience, God's Spirit striving with the sinner but not forever, if man persists the Spirit ceasing to strive and judgment coming); Ellicott notes the alternative rendering 'abide'/'dwell' — 'my spirit shall not always abide (remain) in man' (the breath/life of God not remaining in man forever), though 'strive' (contend) is well supported, the two renderings differing on whether the Spirit contends with man or the life-breath remains in him; 'for that he also is flesh' (man fallen, carnal, given over to fleshly corruption, his being 'flesh' why the Spirit will not always strive); Henry and Gill read 'his days shall be an hundred and twenty years' as a respite, a reprieve, a space of repentance before the flood (God's longsuffering granting 120 years' warning, during which Noah built the ark and preached, that men might repent, the period of probation), while a second reading takes the 120 years as the new limit on human lifespan (man's life henceforth shortened to 120 years).

debates.question: What is the 'hundred and twenty years' (and the meaning of 'strive')?

sides.position: A respite/space of repentance before the flood — 120 years of God's longsuffering and warning; the Spirit 'striving' (contending) by conscience and Noah's preaching
The new limit on human lifespan — man's life henceforth shortened to 120 years; 'strive' rendered 'abide/remain' (the breath not remaining in man)

themes: My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years
God's word that his Spirit shall not always strive, and the hundred and twenty years
the crux of the period (a respite before the flood vs the new limit on lifespan)
the Spirit striving (contending) or abiding (remaining)

schools.summary: The giants and mighty men: 'there were giants in the earth in those days; and also after that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bare children to them, the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown' — the 'Nephilim' (variously rendered 'giants,' or 'fallen ones,' or 'violent ones') in the earth in those days (men of great stature, strength, or violence, whether literal giants or mighty, violent men, their presence marking the age of corruption); the offspring of the union of the sons of God and the daughters of men (v.2) becoming mighty men (on the angelic view, the Nephilim the offspring of angels and women; on the Sethite view, the mighty men the offspring of the mixed marriages, the union producing a race of powerful, renowned men); 'mighty men which were of old, men of renown (men of name)' (famous, powerful men, renowned for their strength, exploits, or more likely their violence and tyranny, 'men of renown' not for virtue but for might and infamy, the heroes of the corrupt age, whose violence filled the earth, v.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Henry.

themes: There were giants in the earth in those days... the same became mighty men which were of old, men of renown
the giants and mighty men of old
the Nephilim, men of great stature, strength, or violence
the powerful, renowned men of the corrupt age

schools.summary: The total corruption of man: 'and God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually' — God beholding the vast, universal wickedness of mankind (the corruption become enormous and widespread, the whole race, save Noah, sunk in sin, God's all-seeing eye marking the depth of human depravity before the flood); 'every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually' the searching description (the very imaginations and thoughts of man's heart wholly evil, and that without ceasing, the corruption total — every imagination — exclusive — only evil — and constant — continually — the strongest statement of human depravity, the heart's thoughts wholly and always evil, the root of sin in the corrupt heart, cf. Jeremiah 17:9; Matthew 15:19); the total depravity of man's heart the ground of the coming judgment (the wickedness not occasional or partial but pervasive and inward, the corruption of the heart issuing in the violence that filled the earth, v.11, the flood to purge a world wholly given over to evil) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Clarke.

themes: God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually
the total corruption of man's heart
the heart wholly, exclusively, and constantly corrupt
the depth of human depravity, the ground of the coming judgment

schools.summary: God's repentance: 'and it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart' — 'it repented the LORD' not a change in God's eternal mind or purpose (for God does not change, Numbers 23:19; 1 Samuel 15:29; Malachi 3:6), but spoken 'by an anthropopathy, after the manner of men' (God's 'repentance' expressing his real displeasure at man's sin and his changed dealing toward the race, from favor to judgment, God speaking of himself as men do that we may understand his abhorrence of sin and his resolve to judge); God's repenting marking the greatness of man's sin and the change in God's outward dispensation, not in his unchanging counsel (his 'repentance' his grief and displeasure, leading to the resolve to destroy man, v.7, the language accommodating the divine to human understanding); 'it grieved him at his heart' (God represented as grieved, pained at heart, by man's wickedness, the figure expressing God's holy displeasure and sorrow over sin, not that God suffers as men do but that sin is truly hateful to him, the strong anthropomorphism marking how deeply sin offends the holy God) — Calvin, Gill, Henry, Keil.

themes: it repented the LORD that he had made man on the earth, and it grieved him at his heart
God's repentance and grief over man (an anthropomorphism)
not a change in God's eternal purpose but his holy displeasure at sin
sin truly hateful to and grieving the holy God

schools.summary: God's resolve to destroy: 'and the LORD said, I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air; for it repenteth me that I have made them' — God resolving to destroy (blot out) mankind, whom he had made, blotting them from the earth (the corruption, v.5, having reached its term, the Creator judging his creature, the sentence of universal destruction pronounced); not only man but 'beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air' included in the destruction (the creatures made for man's use perishing with him, the animal creation, though innocent, sharing in the judgment, since they were made for man and are involved in his ruin, the whole order of the earth swept away with sinful man); 'for it repenteth me that I have made them' (the anthropopathy, v.6, repeated, marking God's displeasure as the ground of the judgment, God's 'repentance' his resolve to destroy what sin has corrupted, the grief over sin issuing in the sentence of destruction) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: I will destroy man whom I have created from the face of the earth; both man, and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air
God's resolve to destroy man and the creatures
the whole order of the earth swept away with sinful man
God's displeasure at sin the ground of the judgment

schools.summary: Noah found grace: 'but Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD' — over against the universal corruption and the resolved destruction (vv.5-7), Noah finding grace (the 'but' marking the exception, amid a world doomed to judgment one man finding favor with God, the gracious preservation of Noah and, through him, of the race and the line of promise); Noah 'found grace (favor)' in God's eyes (not earned by merit but the free favor of God, Noah's righteousness, v.9, itself the fruit of grace, God's grace singling out Noah for salvation from the flood, the doctrine of salvation by grace amid universal judgment); 'in the eyes of the LORD' (God looking on Noah with favor, the same God who saw the wickedness of man, v.5, seeing Noah with grace, Noah the type of the remnant saved by grace, the one godly man preserved when the world perishes, grace marking the survival of the line of promise) — Ellicott, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: But Noah found grace in the eyes of the LORD
Noah finding grace amid the judgment
the free favor of God, the remnant saved by grace
one godly man preserved when the world perishes

schools.summary: Noah's character: 'these are the generations of Noah: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God' — the formula 'these are the generations (toledoth) of Noah' introducing the account of Noah and the flood (chs. 6-9, the history turning to Noah, the one righteous man, and his preservation); 'Noah was a just (righteous) man and perfect (blameless, upright) in his generations' (Noah righteous, justified by faith, Hebrews 11:7, and blameless in his conduct, 'perfect' not as sinless but as upright and sincere, complete in integrity, 'in his generations' — amid a corrupt and wicked age — Noah standing out as righteous, his integrity shining against the darkness of his times); 'Noah walked with God' (like Enoch, 5:24, Noah walking with God in close fellowship, faith, and obedience, his life a walk of communion with God, Noah's righteousness, integrity, and walk with God the fruit of the grace he found, v.8, and the ground of his preservation) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Henry.

themes: Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God
Noah's character - just, perfect, and walking with God
righteous by faith and blameless amid a corrupt age
Noah's walk with God like Enoch's, the fruit of grace

schools.summary: Noah's three sons: 'and Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth' — Noah's three sons (named, as in 5:32), from whom the whole post-flood race would descend (the family of Noah, who alone would be preserved through the flood, the eight souls — Noah, his wife, his three sons, and their wives, 1 Peter 3:20 — saved in the ark); Shem, Ham, and Japheth the three sons through whom the earth would be repeopled (9:18-19; 10:1, the whole human race after the flood springing from them, the line of promise continuing especially through Shem); Noah's family — himself, his wife, his three sons, and their wives — the remnant preserved in the ark (God preserving not Noah only but his household, through this one family the race and the line of promise surviving the flood, the household of the righteous saved with him) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Noah begat three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth
Noah's three sons
the heads of the post-flood race, the eight souls preserved in the ark
the line of promise continuing through Shem

schools.summary: The earth corrupt and violent: 'the earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence' — the earth (its inhabitants) corrupt, depraved, and ruined in God's sight (the corruption not hidden but 'before God,' open to his all-seeing eye, the whole earth morally rotten, the corruption that began with the intermarriages, v.2, having spread to fill the earth); 'the earth was filled with violence' (the corruption of the heart, v.5, issuing in violence: oppression, injustice, bloodshed, and wrong, the strong oppressing the weak, the mighty men, v.4, filling the earth with their violence, the two great marks of the age corruption — toward God — and violence — toward man — the breach of both tables of the law); the earth's corruption and violence the ground of the flood (the whole earth, given over to depravity and violence, ripe for judgment, God's longsuffering, the 120 years, v.3, exhausted, the corrupt, violent world to be purged) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: The earth also was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence
the earth corrupt and filled with violence
corruption toward God and violence toward man, the two marks of the age
the corrupt, violent world ripe for judgment

schools.summary: God beholds the corruption: 'and God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth' — God surveying the earth and seeing it corrupt (the 'behold' marking the certainty and the gravity of what God saw, God's inspection confirming the corruption, the all-seeing God beholding the depravity that fills the earth, and judging righteously on the basis of what he sees); 'all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth' (all mankind, all flesh, save Noah and his house, having corrupted their way, the corruption universal, every man having departed from the right way, 'corrupted his way' marking the perversion of conduct, the whole manner of life depraved, the universal apostasy and depravity of the race); 'all flesh' the whole race corrupt, and, in their ruin, the creatures (none exempt save the household of Noah, the universal extent of the corruption the ground of the universal judgment, the whole earth, save the ark, given over to sin) — Ellicott, Henry, Gill.

themes: God looked upon the earth, and, behold, it was corrupt; for all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth
God beholding the universal corruption of all flesh
every man's way perverted, the universal apostasy of the race
the universal corruption the ground of the universal judgment

schools.summary: God reveals the judgment to Noah: 'and God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth' — God revealing to Noah his purpose: the end (the destruction) of all flesh determined before him (God confiding his counsel to the righteous Noah, cf. Amos 3:7, 'the Lord God will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets,' the doom of the corrupt race announced to the one man who found grace); 'for the earth is filled with violence through them' (the violence of mankind, v.11, the ground of the judgment, the corruption and violence that fill the earth calling for its destruction, God's judgment just, answering to the universal wickedness); 'I will destroy them with the earth' (God to destroy mankind together with the earth, its surface, the world they corrupted, the flood to sweep away both the corrupt race and the world they had defiled, the universal destruction announced to Noah, who is then commanded to build the ark, v.14, for his preservation) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth
God revealing to Noah the coming destruction of all flesh
the violence the ground of the judgment, the race destroyed with the earth
the doom revealed to the righteous Noah

schools.summary: The command to build the ark: 'make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch' — God commanding Noah to build an ark (a chest or vessel) of gopher wood (a resinous, durable timber, perhaps cypress, the ark the means of Noah's preservation, God providing the way of salvation from the judgment he announces); 'rooms (nests, cells) shalt thou make in the ark' (the ark to have many compartments or chambers, to house Noah's family, the animals, and the provisions, the careful, divinely-given design of the ark, God instructing Noah in the building); 'shalt pitch it within and without with pitch (kopher)' (the ark to be sealed with pitch, bitumen, inside and out, to make it watertight, the wordplay, kopher, pitch, and kaphar, to cover/atone, leading some to see the ark, sealed and saving, as a type of Christ and salvation, the covering that keeps out the waters of judgment, the ark, well-built and sealed, to bear Noah safely through the flood) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: Make thee an ark of gopher wood; rooms shalt thou make in the ark, and shalt pitch it within and without with pitch
the command to build the ark
the saving vessel divinely designed and sealed
the ark the means of Noah's preservation through the flood

schools.summary: The dimensions of the ark: 'and this is the fashion which thou shalt make it of: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth of it fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits' — God giving Noah the exact dimensions and design of the ark (the building by divine instruction, not human invention, the ark made according to God's pattern); the dimensions three hundred cubits long, fifty broad, thirty high (about 450 by 75 by 45 feet, taking the cubit at 18 inches, an immense vessel with a vast capacity, the proportions — long, relatively narrow, and of moderate height — suited for stability and floating, not for sailing, the great size needed to house the family, the animals of every kind, and the provisions); the ark's huge dimensions giving ample room for the pairs of all the kinds of animals and their food (the calculation of its capacity showing it sufficient for the purpose, the divinely-given design fit for its task, God's wisdom in the proportions of the ark) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Clarke, Gill.

themes: The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth of it fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits
the dimensions of the ark
the immense vessel of ample capacity, built by God's pattern
fit to house the family, the animals, and the provisions

schools.summary: The window, door, and stories: 'a window shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above; and the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof; with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it' — 'a window (an opening for light) shalt thou make to the ark, and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above' (the ark to have a window or opening for light and air, 'in a cubit shalt thou finish it above' variously understood — the window finished to within a cubit of the top, or the roof sloping to a cubit — the ark provided with light from above); 'the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof' (a single door in the side, by which Noah, his family, and the animals would enter, the one door of entrance, which God himself would shut, 7:16, some seeing a type of Christ, the one door of salvation, John 10:9); 'with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it' (the ark built with three stories or decks, providing ample, ordered space for the family, the animals, and the provisions, the three levels giving room and arrangement for all that must be preserved, the careful, divinely-given structure of the ark) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: A window shalt thou make to the ark... and the door of the ark shalt thou set in the side thereof; with lower, second, and third stories shalt thou make it
the window, door, and three stories of the ark
the ark fitted with light, a single door of entrance, and three ordered decks
the careful, divinely-given structure of the ark

schools.summary: God announces the flood: 'and, behold, I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die' — God emphatically declaring that he himself will bring the flood (mabbul) of waters upon the earth (the doubled 'I, even I' marking the divine agency and certainty, the flood God's own act of judgment, not a natural accident, God bringing the waters to destroy); the flood to 'destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven' (all living creatures, men and animals, that have the breath of life perishing, the destruction universal — all flesh under heaven — the breath of life, given by God, taken, the comprehensive sweep of the judgment); 'every thing that is in the earth shall die' (all that lived on the earth, save those in the ark, dying in the flood, the totality of the destruction, the corrupt world and all its living creatures swept away, the universal death announced as the judgment on a world given over to sin) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die
God's announcement of the flood to destroy all flesh
God's own act of universal judgment by the flood
all living creatures swept away save those in the ark

schools.summary: God's covenant with Noah: 'but with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee' — God making a covenant with Noah (the first explicit mention of 'covenant,' berith, in Scripture, God binding himself to preserve Noah and his house through the flood, and afterward, the covenant of 9:9-17, the covenant of grace and preservation amid the judgment, God's gracious engagement to save Noah); 'thou shalt come into the ark' (the ark the appointed means of Noah's preservation, Noah to enter it by faith and obedience, the covenant fulfilled through the ark, salvation from the flood by entering the divinely-provided refuge); 'thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee' (the covenant embracing Noah's household, the eight souls, 1 Peter 3:20, preserved in the ark, God saving not Noah only but his family, the household of the righteous preserved with him, the covenant of grace extending to Noah's house, through whom the race would be renewed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: with thee will I establish my covenant; and thou shalt come into the ark, thou, and thy sons, and thy wife, and thy sons' wives with thee
God's covenant with Noah and the preservation of his household
the first mention of covenant, the covenant of preservation amid judgment
the eight souls saved through the divinely-provided refuge

schools.summary: The gathering of the animals: 'and of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee; they shall be male and female' — God commanding Noah to bring two of every kind of living creature into the ark, to preserve them alive through the flood (the animals, which would otherwise perish, v.17, to be preserved in the ark, God's care extending to the preservation of the animal kinds); 'two of every sort' (a pair, male and female, of each kind, to be brought into the ark, the basic preservation of each species by a breeding pair, the clean by sevens, 7:2, the kinds preserved for the repopulation of the earth after the flood); 'they shall be male and female' (male and female of each kind, that the species might be propagated and the earth restocked after the flood, God's provision for the continuance of the animal creation, the pairs preserved to renew the kinds, the divine care for the whole creation, not man only) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: of every living thing of all flesh, two of every sort shalt thou bring into the ark, to keep them alive with thee; they shall be male and female
the gathering of the animals, two of every sort, into the ark
the breeding pairs preserved to renew the kinds
God's care for the whole creation, not man only

schools.summary: The kinds come to Noah: 'of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive' — the three classes of land creatures (fowls, cattle, creeping things), each after its kind, coming to Noah, two of every sort, to be preserved (the comprehensive gathering of the animal kinds, each species represented by a pair); the repeated 'after their kind' (each species distinct, gathered by kind, the preservation of the distinct kinds, the species created in ch. 1, the ark to hold representatives of all the land-dwelling, air-breathing creatures, that the kinds might survive); 'two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive' (notably, the animals would 'come unto' Noah, God himself, by his providence, bringing the creatures to the ark, the animals coming by divine instinct and direction, not all hunted out by Noah, God's hand in gathering the kinds, the creatures preserved alive by God's provision) — Gill.

themes: Of fowls after their kind, and of cattle after their kind, of every creeping thing of the earth after his kind, two of every sort shall come unto thee, to keep them alive
the kinds of creatures coming to Noah to be preserved
the comprehensive gathering of the distinct animal kinds
the animals brought to the ark by God's providence

schools.summary: The gathering of food: 'and take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them' — Noah to gather food of every kind, stored in the ark, to feed himself, his family, and the animals during the flood (the provision of food for the long months in the ark, God's command extending to the practical preservation of life through the flood); 'of all food that is eaten' (every kind of food suitable for man and beast, the varied provision needed to sustain the family and the many animals, the gathering and storing of sufficient food for all in the ark); 'it shall be for food for thee, and for them' (the stored food to sustain both Noah's household and the animals through the months of the flood, God's care providing not only the ark, the refuge, but the food, the sustenance, the complete provision for the preservation of life, Noah's obedience in gathering the food part of the faith by which he prepared the ark, Hebrews 11:7) — Gill.

themes: take thou unto thee of all food that is eaten, and thou shalt gather it to thee; and it shall be for food for thee, and for them
the gathering of food for the ark
the sustenance stored for the months of the flood
God's care providing both the refuge and the food

schools.summary: Noah's complete obedience: 'thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he' — Noah doing as God commanded (setting about the great work of building the ark, gathering the animals and food, in obedience to God, Noah's faith showing itself in works, Hebrews 11:7, 'by faith Noah... prepared an ark,' his obedience to the seemingly strange command — building a great ship on dry land, against the scorn of the world — the fruit of his faith); 'according to all that God commanded him' (his obedience complete, exact, and unreserved, he doing all that God commanded, not part only, the full, careful obedience of faith, Noah holding back nothing but doing everything as God directed, the pattern of thorough obedience); 'so did he' (the emphatic repetition stressing that Noah actually did it, his faith not bare profession but active obedience, he building the ark and making all the preparation over the long years, the 120 years, v.3, enduring the scorn of the wicked, Noah's obedient faith the model of the believer who, warned of things not seen, prepares for salvation) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Thus did Noah; according to all that God commanded him, so did he
Noah's complete obedience
the active faith that built the ark and made all preparation
the model of the believer warned of things not seen (Hebrews 11:7)
